questions.text: I find that my appetite, sleep, and energy are rarely affected by emotional events.
I trust my gut instincts more than any amount of advance preparation.
I can tell when someone is interested in a conversation topic versus enduring it out of courtesy.
I feel that my flexibility is one of my greatest assets.
I believe that nurturing my own potential is one of my most important responsibilities.
I think that structured, logical engagement with human problems is a more reliable path to justice than empathic immersion.
I focus on creating the conditions for a great present and let the future emerge from that.
I believe that reading too much into people's behavior leads to misunderstandings.
I do not naturally track who is allied with whom in a social group.
I notice that I frequently steer conversations toward inclusion.
I feel most alive when I am steering the course of a project.
I set aside time regularly to review what's working and what needs adjustment.
I feel that my personal comfort matters less than the comfort of those around me.
I believe that the moral force of suffering is what makes ethical reasoning necessary rather than optional.
I notice that my emotional responses are like ripples on a pond rather than crashing waves.
I believe that the ability to examine suffering without flinching — to hold it at a distance and assess it — is essential for meaningful intervention.
My internal emotional weather is rarely calm — there is almost always a storm or a brilliant sun.
I feel that maintaining a strong sense of self requires consistently investing in myself.
